1.pericope_label: Prologue: The Unveiling of Jesus Christ
1.exposition: Revelation opens by naming itself — apokalypsis, an unveiling — and by naming the chain down which the unveiling travels: from God, to Jesus Christ, to his angel, to his servant John, and out to the churches. The book is not a puzzle-box of the future but a disclosure of what was hidden, and its first blessing falls not on the decoder but on the one who reads aloud, and those who hear and keep — a document for the worshiping assembly, meant to be obeyed rather than solved. The epistolary greeting then frames the whole as a letter to seven churches in Asia, carrying grace and peace from the eternal Father, the sevenfold Spirit, and Jesus Christ under three titles — the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, the ruler of the kings of the earth. That threefold name cannot hold its form and breaks at once into doxology; and the prologue closes as God himself speaks the only line he utters in the chapter: I am the Alpha and the Omega. Before a single seal is opened, the reader is told who governs time.
1.original_language: The book's title-word is apokalypsis (v.1) — an uncovering, the drawing back of a veil, not the encoding of a secret. What it shows "must soon take place" (en tachei), for "the time is near" (engys, v.3) — read either as imminence or as the swiftness with which the events run once they begin. The climactic v.8 names God by the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, Alpha and Ō (Omega): he is beginning and end, and the whole alphabet of history lies between his two hands. The self-designation "who is and who was and who is to come" bends grammar on purpose — where one expects "who will be," John writes "who is coming," so that God's future is named not as bare existence but as advent.
1.historical_context: By the late first century apocalyptic was a well-worn Jewish genre — Daniel, 1 Enoch, 4 Ezra — and John's readers in Roman Asia would have known its conventions. But the prologue's titles are also pointed counter-imperial claims. Domitian is reported to have welcomed the address Dominus et Deus noster, "our Lord and God"; to call Jesus "the ruler of the kings of the earth" (v.5) is to say, quietly and unmistakably, that Caesar is not. The "seven churches which are in Asia" (v.4) sat on a roughly circular road running north from Ephesus and back inland — a courier's circuit — so the Apocalypse is a genuine circular letter. And the doxology's "kingdom, priests to his God" (v.6) lifts Exodus 19:6, Israel's charter at Sinai, and lays it on these small and pressured congregations.
1.christ: The prologue is already dense with Christ. The three titles of v.5 trace an arc: faithful witness — his earthly testimony, sealed at the cross, the martys whose faithfulness his people are called to share; firstborn of the dead — his resurrection, the first of a great harvest; and ruler of the kings of the earth — his present cosmic reign. The doxology that follows, "to him who loves us and freed us from our sins by his blood" (v.6), grounds the whole book pastorally: the churches are the already-loved and already-freed. Most striking, the "Alpha and Omega… the Almighty" of v.8, spoken here by God, is claimed by the risen Christ himself at the book's close (Revelation 22:13). What God says of himself, Jesus will say of himself.

verses.note: The first word governs the book: the revelation (apokalypsis) of Jesus Christ, which God gave him. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace the chain of disclosure — God to Christ, Christ to his angel, the angel to John, John to the servants — and read apokalypsis as an unveiling of what was hidden, a book belonging to Christ and given him to show. That it was "signified" (esēmanen, shown by signs) is pressed by the latin-fathers Victorinus of Pettau and Bede: the revelation comes clothed in symbol, and the reader is warned from the outset not to flatten its images into plain prose. The genitive runs both ways — a revelation of Christ, and about him; he is at once revealer and revealed.

John bore witness to the word of God and to the testimony of Jesus Christ, even to all that he saw. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the two phrases name the whole substance of his record — the divine message and the witness Jesus bore — the very pairing that will return as the ground of his exile (v.9). The apostle is the faithful recorder "of all things that he saw": he does not edit the vision but testifies to it. Witness (martyria) is already the book's keyword, and John embodies it before he commends it.

The first of the book's seven beatitudes: blessed is the one who reads aloud, and those who hear and keep the words of this prophecy. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson picture the setting — a single lector reading to the gathered congregation — so the blessing rests on the public reader and the assembly together. But puritan Matthew Henry presses the last verb: the blessing belongs especially to those who keep what is written, for the book is given not to satisfy curiosity but to be obeyed. The ground of the urgency is that "the time is near" — near in God's reckoning, and pressing on the hearer now.

The letter opens: grace and peace from him who is and who was and who is to come, and from the seven Spirits before his throne. The first title is the eternal God, the everlasting I AM in past, present, and future. Over "the seven Spirits" the witnesses divide: reformed Gill, with the traditional Trinitarian reading, takes it as the one Holy Spirit under a sevenfold figure of fullness — the placement between the Father and Christ (v.5) strongly favoring it; grammatical-historical Barnes inclines rather to seven angels of the presence that stand before the throne. That the churches are seven — the number of completeness — hints they stand for the whole church.

Three titles crown the greeting: the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott read them as a sweep from Christ's faithful testimony (even unto death), through his resurrection as the pledge of ours, to his sovereignty over every earthly throne. At the naming of Christ the sentence breaks into doxology — "to him who loves us and washed us from our sins in his own blood." The verb is present tense: he loves us still, and the cleansing is already accomplished in his blood. Redeeming love, not bare power, is the ground of the praise.

Redemption issues in dignity: Christ made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear behind it Exodus 19:6 and 1 Peter 2:9: the redeemed share Christ's royalty, reigning with him, and his priesthood, with access to God and spiritual sacrifices to offer. The honor once spoken over Israel at Sinai now falls on churches the empire counts as nothing. And the doxology is sealed: "to him be glory and dominion forever and ever. Amen" — eternal praise rendered to the Redeemer whose love has so exalted his own.

The book's keynote sounds: behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear two prophets fused — the Son of Man coming with clouds of Daniel 7:13 and the pierced one mourned in Zechariah 12:10 — the same fusion Jesus made in Matthew 24:30. The coming is visible and universal: "even those who pierced him," and "all the tribes of the earth will wail," whether in penitence or in terror before the Judge. The double "Even so, Amen" seals the certainty in Greek and Hebrew alike.

God speaks in his own voice, the single such line in the chapter: I am the Alpha and the Omega… the Almighty. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note the sweep of the self-designation — the first and last letters standing for the whole; the eternal one "who is and who was and who is to come"; and Pantokratōr, the all-ruling Almighty, whose omnipotence undergirds every promise the book will make. The title is God's here; at the book's end the risen Christ takes it on his own lips (Revelation 22:13) — an unforced witness to his deity.

witnesses.point: Reads apokalypsis as an unveiling of the hidden, disclosed down a chain from God to Christ to the angel to John; inclines to take the seven Spirits as angels of the presence rather than the Holy Spirit.
The book was shown by signs and symbols, and its opening beatitude promises blessing to the one who reads and hears and keeps — that he may learn from the reading to do the works it commands.
The seven Spirits before the throne are the one Holy Spirit named under a sevenfold figure of fullness, standing in the Trinitarian salutation between the Father and Christ.
The blessing rests especially on those who keep the words of the prophecy, not merely hear them; the book is given for obedience, not curiosity.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The designation 'who is and who was and who is to come' unfolds the divine name and pointedly substitutes 'who is to come' for the expected 'who will be' — God's future is not static existence but his coming to his world.

1.application: The prologue hands the anxious reader a map before the storm: the God who owns the alphabet of history has already loved you and freed you. Whatever the seals unseal, the first and the last word belongs to him.

9.pericope_label: The Vision of the Son of Man
9.exposition: Now the seer steps forward — and he steps forward as a fellow-sufferer. Before he describes the glory, John names himself brother and partner in the tribulation and the kingdom and the patient endurance, exiled to Patmos for the word of God. In the Spirit on the Lord's Day he hears a trumpet-voice behind him, turns, and sees first not the throne of heaven but seven golden lampstands — the churches — with one like a son of man walking among them. The figure is drawn stroke by stroke from Daniel and Ezekiel: hair white as snow, eyes like a flame of fire, feet like burnished bronze, a voice like many waters, a sharp sword from his mouth, a face like the sun in full strength. John falls as though dead; and the same hand that holds the seven stars is laid on him with the word that anchors the whole book — Fear not; I am the first and the last, and the living one, who died and is alive forevermore and holds the keys of Death and Hades. The vision closes by handing the reader its own key (v.20): the lampstands are the churches, the stars their angels.
9.original_language: The central title is homoion huion anthrōpou, "one like a son of man" (v.13) — the figure of Daniel 7:13 who comes with the clouds and receives dominion. Yet the white hair belongs, in Daniel, to the Ancient of Days (Daniel 7:9), and the fiery eyes and gleaming bronze to the heavenly man of Daniel 10:6; the many-waters voice is the voice of God returning to the temple in Ezekiel 43:2. The sword is a rhomphaia, the long broadsword, proceeding from his mouth — his word is his weapon (Isaiah 49:2). "The Lord's Day" (kyriakē hēmera, v.10) is the earliest surviving use of the phrase for Sunday, the adjective identical to that of "the Lord's Supper" (1 Corinthians 11:20).
9.historical_context: Patmos is a small volcanic island in the Aegean, some thirty-seven miles southwest of Ephesus; Rome used such islands for relegatio, the banishment of offenders of standing. John is there "on account of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus" (v.9), which locates the vision within the imperial pressure on the churches, most plausibly under Domitian. The imagery answers that pressure point for point. The proconsul at Pergamum held the ius gladii, the right of the sword — the power to execute; the sword that finally matters, John sees, comes from the mouth of Christ (v.16). And the seven golden lampstands evoke the temple menorah, Israel's light before God, now identified as the seven churches — with Christ himself moving among them.
9.christ: This is one of the New Testament's boldest identifications of Jesus with God. The attributes Daniel divides between the Ancient of Days and the son of man, John gathers onto a single figure: the exalted Christ wears the white hair of the Eternal One even as he bears the human title "son of man." He stands "in the midst" of the lampstands (v.13) — no absentee Lord but the present High Priest in his long robe and golden sash, tending the lamps. His self-declaration binds two claims only God and only the crucified could speak together: "the first and the last" is the divine name of Isaiah 44:6, and "I died, and behold I am alive forevermore" is the confession of the risen Jesus (v.18). Because he holds the keys of Death and Hades, the last enemy the churches face has already been mastered by the one who writes to them.

verses.note: John introduces himself without rank: your brother and partner in the tribulation and the kingdom and the patient endurance that are in Jesus. reformed Gill notes he claims no lordship over them but names himself simply a brother, of the family named from Christ; grammatical-historical Barnes marks the three words held together — tribulation, kingdom, and endurance — as the ordinary shape of Christian life: the reign is entered through affliction, and patience is what bridges them. He was on Patmos "for the word of God," his suffering the very occasion of the vision.

I was in the Spirit on the Lord's Day. grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott take the phrase, with the settled consensus, as Sunday — the Christian Lord's Day named for the resurrection, the adjective the same one used of "the Lord's Supper" (1 Corinthians 11:20) — and expressly reject the futurist reading that would make it the eschatological "day of the Lord." "In the Spirit" names a prophetic rapture, John carried beyond ordinary sense. Then, from behind him, a great voice "as of a trumpet": loud, clear, and commanding attention.

The voice gives its charge: what you see, write in a book and send to the seven churches — then names them in the order of the courier's circuit: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamos, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that the Apocalypse is thereby a single circular letter, though each church will receive its own tailored word (chs. 2–3). The seven actual congregations of proconsular Asia are also, by their number, the whole church addressed.

Turning "to see the voice," John sees first not the speaker but seven golden lampstands. grammatical-historical Barnes explains them by the vision's own key (v.20): they are the seven churches. puritan Matthew Henry draws the sense — the church's office is to hold forth light, not to generate it; the gold marks how precious these lampstands are to Christ, and the seven their completeness. They recall the single temple menorah, now multiplied among the congregations of Asia.

In the midst of the lampstands stands one like a son of man, clothed with a long robe and girded about the breast with a golden sash. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the title from Daniel 7:13 — the glorified, dominion-receiving figure of Daniel's vision — and read the long robe and golden girdle as the dress of dignity and priesthood. That he stands "in the midst" is itself the message: Christ is present with his churches, walking among the lamps he tends, neither absent nor idle.

His head and hair were white like wool, white as snow, and his eyes like a flame of fire. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that the whiteness is lifted straight from the Ancient of Days of Daniel 7:9 — a startling transfer, for what Daniel saw of God is now seen of the Son, marking his eternity and spotless holiness. The flaming eyes signify a gaze nothing escapes: the searching, all-knowing look that will read each church to its depths in the letters to come (cf. Revelation 2:18).

His feet were like burnished bronze, refined in a furnace, and his voice like the roar of many waters. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the glowing feet as strength and stability, and the furnace as the purity of judgment — feet that tread down and cannot be moved. The voice "of many waters" is the voice of God returning to his temple in Ezekiel 43:2: overwhelming and majestic, drowning every lesser sound — including, John's readers would feel, the decrees of Rome.

Three final strokes: in his right hand seven stars, from his mouth a sharp two-edged sword, and his face like the sun shining in full strength. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the stars (the churches' angels, v.20) as held safe in his hand, and the sword from his mouth as his word — the piercing, dividing, judging word of Hebrews 4:12 and Isaiah 49:2, which both saves and smites. The blazing face recalls the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:2) — glory too great for the beholder to bear.

When I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the response is the standard effect of beholding divine majesty, as with Ezekiel 1:28 and Daniel 8:17 — the prophet is unmanned before the glory. Then the tenderness: the hand that held the stars is laid on him with "Fear not." puritan Matthew Henry marks that the same Christ whose glory overwhelms is the one who reassures. And the ground of comfort is a divine name — "I am the first and the last" (Isaiah 44:6) — the Eternal One his own need not dread.

The self-declaration completes: I am the living one; I was dead, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the proof of his identity is the resurrection — the one speaking is the one who died. reformed Gill presses the keys: Christ holds authority over death and the state of the dead, the sovereign disposal of the bodies and souls of men, opening and shutting as he wills. For churches facing the sword, no word could weigh more: death's own keys hang at the belt of the one who writes to them.

The commission is given its shape: write the things you have seen, the things that are, and the things that are to take place after this. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read a threefold outline — the vision just seen (ch. 1), the present state of the churches (chs. 2–3), and the future unveiled thereafter (chs. 4–22). Many take the verse as the book's own table of contents, though its three tenses interweave more than a strict timeline allows.

The vision closes by unlocking itself: the seven stars are the angels of the seven churches, and the seven lampstands are the seven churches. That the lampstands are the churches all agree. Over the "angels" the witnesses divide: the dominant Protestant reading — grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson with reformed Gill — takes angeloi ("messengers") as the human ministers or pastors, each letter addressed to the one who represents his congregation; others read literal guardian angels, or personifications of the churches themselves. Either way, the churches and their leaders are held in Christ's right hand — and the book has taught its method: the symbols are strange, but they are disclosed.

witnesses.point: The vision is a mosaic of Daniel and Ezekiel, and the risen Christ claims 'the first and the last' — a title the prophets reserved for Yahweh; 'the Lord's Day' is Sunday, not the eschatological day of the Lord.

The white hair of the Ancient of Days is transferred to the Son, affirming his eternity; the threefold charge of v.19 supplies the book's outline — the vision, the present churches, and the things to come.
John names himself simply a brother, not a lord over the churches; and the keys of Death and Hades mark Christ's sovereign disposal of the bodies and souls of men.
The lampstands are the churches, precious as gold and set to hold forth light; the same Christ whose glory strikes John down lays his hand on him and says, 'Fear not.'

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The portrait deliberately blends the attributes Daniel assigns to the Ancient of Days (the white hair) with those of the 'one like a son of man' (Daniel 7), identifying the risen Christ with the divine figure of the vision and casting the whole theophany as a mosaic of Daniel and Ezekiel.

9.application: The persecuted churches are shown their Lord before they are shown their trials: he walks among the lamps, holds the stars, and carries death's keys. Whatever Rome can do, it cannot reach past the hand laid on the fallen seer with the words, "Fear not."

_meta.title: The Unveiling of Jesus Christ
reflection: The risen Christ appears with hair white as snow, eyes like fire, and a voice like many waters (vv.14–16). How does this glorified Christ compare with the Jesus you most often hold in mind — and what would change if you let this picture correct that one?

To churches facing the sword, Christ says, "Fear not… I have the keys of Death and Hades" (vv.17–18). What fear would loosen its grip if you truly believed that the one who holds death's keys is the one who has laid his hand on you?

The book's first blessing is for those who read, hear, and keep its words (v.3). Do you approach Revelation as a puzzle to be solved or as a prophecy to be obeyed — and what would it look like to "keep" it?
